title: Dynamical dimer structure factor of the triangular $S=1/2$ Heisenberg antiferromagnet
authors: Markus Drescher, Laurens Vanderstraeten, Roderich Moessner, Frank Pollmann, Johannes Knolle
abstract: The dynamical dimer structure factor is an observable probing spin-singlet excitations of quantum magnets distinct from those commonly studied by the spin structure factor. We report the dimer response for the extended spin-$1/2$ antiferromagnetic Heisenberg model on the triangular lattice using large-scale GPU-accelerated matrix-product-state simulations. We investigate the ordered phases with $120^\circ$ coplanar, collinear stripe, and tetrahedral spin order, as well as candidate quantum spin-liquid (QSL) regimes, comprising an expected gapless $U(1)$ Dirac QSL and a chiral QSL at finite spin-scalar-chirality coupling. In the ordered phases, we find low-energy modes below the onset of the two-magnon continuum illustrating avoided quasiparticle decay. Within the candidate gapless QSL, we observe absolute dispersion minima at momenta of half the Brillouin zone corners, $X\equiv K/2$, in agreement with field-theory predictions that singlet monopole excitations of the $U(1)$ Dirac spin liquid become gapless at these points. Thus, the high-resolution dynamical dimer response provides support for a $U(1)$ Dirac QSL with singlet monopole excitations.
summary: This paper investigates the dynamical dimer structure factor in the triangular-lattice Heisenberg antiferromagnet to distinguish between various magnetic phases. Using high-resolution GPU-accelerated tensor network simulations, the authors probe spin-singlet excitations that are otherwise invisible in standard spin-spin correlations. They provide evidence for singlet monopole excitations in the U(1) Dirac spin liquid and observe avoided quasiparticle decay in ordered phases. The results serve as a theoretical guide for future experimental studies using techniques like RIXS.
main_problem: The study aims to resolve the spin-singlet excitation spectrum in frustrated quantum magnets, which is difficult to probe using standard spin structure factors, to better understand candidate quantum spin liquid (QSL) phases.
main_result: The researchers identify low-energy modes in ordered phases due to avoided quasiparticle decay and find evidence for gapless singlet monopole excitations in the U(1) Dirac spin liquid phase at the X points of the Brillouin zone.
method: Large-scale GPU-accelerated Matrix-Product-State (MPS) simulations using iDMRG and time evolution, supplemented by Linear Spin-Wave Theory (LSWT) for comparison.
model_or_system: An extended spin-1/2 Heisenberg antiferromagnet on a triangular lattice, including nearest-neighbor (J1), next-nearest-neighbor (J2), and scalar chiral (Jchi) interactions.
key_observables: Dynamical dimer structure factor S^D(k, omega), static dimer structure factor, and spin structure factor.
important_parameters: Exchange couplings J1, J2, and Jchi; bond dimension chi = 2000; YC6 cylinder geometry.
main_assumptions: The study is subject to finite-size effects due to the cylinder geometry, which prevents full resolution of the gapless continuum in the 2D limit.
figures_summary: Figures show the dynamical dimer structure factor for tetrahedral and stripe phases, comparisons between MPS and LSWT, the static dimer structure factor across various phases, and spatial configurations of tetrahedral spin order.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of probing singlet sectors, describes the Hamiltonian and numerical MPS methods, presents results for ordered phases (120, stripe, tetrahedral) and QSL candidates (U(1) Dirac, chiral), compares findings with LSWT, and discusses experimental implications for RIXS and Raman scattering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic phase diagram of the extended Heisen- berg model on the triangular lattice with a scalar chiral inter- action. The qualitative phase boundaries follow previous nu- merical studies (cf. Ref. [44, 45]). The inset within the chiral spin-liquid regime shows the triangular lattice with Bravais vectors a1 = (1, 0)T and a2 = (1/2, √

FIG. 2. The static dimer structure factor χD(k) for the various ordered and liquid phases considered in this work. The left column shows the results for the 120○phase at J2 = 0 from MPS (top) and LSWT (bottom), the following column for the tetrahedral phase for J2/J1 = 0.125 and Jχ/J1 = 0.5 (MPS, c, LSWT, d), the third column for the stripe order with J2/J1 = 0.55 (MPS, e, spin-wave result, f). The last column shows the numerical data for the candidate J1 −J2 QSL (g) and the chiral spin liquid (h). The former ground state at J2/J1 = 0.125 (Jχ = 0) was stabilized in the odd (low-energy) sector (cf. End Matter), the ground state in the CSL for J2/J1 = 0.125 together with Jχ/J1 = 0.2...

Figure 3a–d presents the dynamical dimer structure factor SD(k,ω) in the 120○phase. The main panels show the data from the MPS-based time evolution, con- trasted with the dynamical spin-wave result in the corre-

FIG. 3. Dynamical dimer structure factor SD(k, ω) for the 120○order (a–d) and the J1 −J2 QSL (e–h). The chosen coupling parameters are J2 = 0 and J2/J1 = 0.125 (both Jχ = 0), respectively. The main panels show the results obtained from numerical time evolutions performed on YC6 cylinders with an MPS bond dimension of χ = 2000 and a cylinder length of Lx = 51. The simulations reached the maximum evolution time of tsim = 60 J1 in steps of δt = 0.04 J1. The structure factors displayed were obtained for a Gaussian broadening σ = 11.4 J1. The insets in panels a–c for the 120○order feature the dimer spectral function along the corresponding cuts as computed within leading-order spin-wave theory....

FIG. 4. The dynamical dimer structure factor in the tetra- hedrally ordered phase at Jχ/J1 = 0.5 and J2/J1 = 0.125. The left column shows the results from dynamical MPS simula- tions at bond dimension χ = 2000 on a YC6 cylinder of size 6 × 81. The maximum evolution time was tsim = 60 J1 with a time-step size of δt = 0.04 J1. The right-hand column presents the leading-order dynamical spin-wave results obtained on a cylinder of dimensions 6×220. The spectral function was com- puted with a Gaussian broadening determined by α = 10−6. The blue lines indicate the three magnon modes arising from the various ground state configurations (cf. the section on spin-wave theory).

FIG. 5. The dynamical dimer structure factor in the stripe phase at J2/J1 = 0.55 (Jχ = 0). The spectral func- tion cuts from the large-scale MPS numerics is displayed on the left. The simulation was performed at a bond dimension of χ = 2000 on a YC6 cylinder with Lx = 126. The dynam- ical parameters are identical to in Fig. 4. The right column shows the corresponding result from the lowest non-vanishing order in spin-wave theory. It was computed on an equivalent cylinder of size 6×180. The blue dashed line marks the single- magnon dispersion as obtained within LSWT.

Figure 6 summarizes the findings for the dynamical dimer structure factor in the chiral spin-liquid phase at Jχ/J1 = 0.2 (J2/J1 = 0.125). The chiral spin liquid is an inherently gapped phase [42, 103]. Accordingly, the lower edge of the excitation continuum along M–M ′

FIG. 6. The dynamical dimer structure factor in the chiral spin-liquid phase. The chosen coupling parameters are J2/J1 = 0.125 and Jχ/J1 = 0.2. The main panels show the results obtained from numerical time evolutions performed on YC6 cylinders with an MPS bond dimension of χ = 2000 and a cylinder length of Lx = 81. The simulations were performed up to tsim = 60 J1 in steps of δt = 0.04 J1. We applied a Gaussian broadening of σ = 11.4 J1. The red dot-dashed line marks the excitation dispersion obtained from the MPS quasiparticle ansatz.

FIG. 7. Profile plots of the static dimer structure factor χD(k) for the various phases considered in this work. For the ordered ground states, we present also the leading-order spin-wave result as a dashed line of the corresponding color. The data displayed and hence also the parameter choices are identical to Fig. 2 in the main text.
